section.title: The Remnant of Israel
section.sectionRationale: Having shown that Israel stumbled over the stone of Christ and was largely disobedient, Paul now asks the burning question: has God then rejected His people? His answer is an emphatic no. Paul himself is living proof, an Israelite of the tribe of Benjamin. As in Elijah's dark day, when the prophet thought he stood alone, God had kept seven thousand for Himself, so now at the present time there is a remnant chosen by grace. And if it is by grace, it cannot be by works, or grace would cease to be grace. The rest were hardened, given eyes that do not see.
section.thesis: God has not rejected the people He foreknew; He has always preserved a remnant chosen by grace, and grace by its very nature excludes works, so that salvation stands on God's free choice and not on human achievement, while the rest are hardened in their unbelief.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the question answered, God has not rejected His people, and Paul is the proof; the pattern of the remnant, as in Elijah's day so now, a remnant chosen by grace and not by works; and the hardening of the rest, eyes that do not see.

units.label: Has God rejected His people?
units.note: certainly not, Paul himself is the proof

verses.text: I ask then, did God reject His people? Certainly not! I am an Israelite myself, a descendant of Abraham, from the tribe of Benjamin.
God did not reject His people, whom He foreknew. Do you not know what the Scripture says about Elijah, how he appealed to God against Israel:
“Lord, they have killed Your prophets and torn down Your altars. I am the only one left, and they are seeking my life as well”?

units.label: A remnant chosen by grace
units.note: as in Elijah's day, and if by grace then not by works

verses.text: And what was the divine reply to him? “I have reserved for Myself seven thousand men who have not bowed the knee to Baal.”
In the same way, at the present time there is a {{leimma|remnant}} chosen by {{charis|grace}}.
And if it is by grace, then it is no longer by works. Otherwise, grace would no longer be grace.

units.label: The rest were hardened
units.note: eyes that do not see, ears that do not hear

verses.text: What then? What Israel was seeking, it failed to obtain, but the {{ekloge|elect}} did. The others were {{poroo|hardened}},
as it is written: “God gave them a spirit of stupor, eyes that could not see, and ears that could not hear, to this very day.”
And David says: “May their table become a snare and a trap, a stumbling block and a retribution to them.
May their eyes be darkened so they cannot see, and their backs be bent forever.”

leimma.gloss: A remnant, what is left over, those who remain. Drawn from the prophets, where a faithful remnant of Israel always survived judgment, the word names the small company God keeps for Himself. It is not the impressive majority but the preserved few, and their existence proves God has not cast off His people but is quietly keeping His promise.

charis.gloss: Grace, unmerited favor, God's free gift. The remnant is chosen by grace, not gathered by human merit. Grace is the sovereign kindness of God that gives what could never be earned; it is the ground on which the remnant stands and the very opposite of works.

ekloge.gloss: Election, choice, selection. Paul says the elect obtained what Israel as a whole sought in vain. The word points to God's gracious choosing, His decision to set His love on a people, so that those who obtain righteousness do so because God first chose them, not because they first chose Him.

poroo.gloss: To harden, to make callous, to render dull or insensible. The others were hardened, their hearts made unresponsive so that eyes do not see and ears do not hear. This hardening is a real and sobering judgment, yet Paul will show it is neither total nor final, for God's purpose of mercy runs beneath even this.

basic.context: Paul has just described Israel largely refusing the gospel and stumbling over Christ. Now he asks whether this means God has abandoned His ancient people altogether. His answer is a firm no. He points to himself, a true Israelite, and then to the days of Elijah, when the prophet felt utterly alone but God had kept seven thousand faithful. So it is now: God has preserved a remnant, and He has done so by grace, not by anything they achieved.
basic.hermeneutics: Read this as the opening of Paul's answer to a fear his argument might raise: if Israel has largely fallen away, has God's word failed? Paul appeals to the Old Testament pattern, Elijah on Mount Horeb, to show that a faithful remnant is exactly how God has always worked. Verse 6 is the hinge: grace and works are mutually exclusive as grounds of standing before God. Verses 8 to 10 gather Scripture to describe the hardening of the rest.
basic.originalLanguages: The key words are leimma, the remnant God keeps for Himself; charis, the grace by which they are chosen; ekloge, God's electing choice, so that the elect obtain what the nation as a whole missed; and poroo, the hardening that came upon the others, dulling their eyes and ears.
basic.typology: Elijah's seven thousand who never bowed to Baal foreshadow the remnant of grace in every age. The faithful few preserved through judgment point to the church, kept not by their strength but by God's choice, and ultimately to Christ, the true and faithful Israelite in whom the remnant is gathered.
basic.christ: Paul the Israelite is himself a trophy of grace, saved by the risen Christ who met him on the Damascus road. The remnant is chosen in Christ and by the grace that comes through Him. What Israel sought, a right standing with God, is found only in Christ, obtained by the elect through faith in Him and never by works.
basic.god: God is faithful; He has not rejected the people He foreknew. He is sovereign in grace, reserving a remnant for Himself just as He reserved seven thousand in Elijah's day. He is also just; the hardening of the rest is His righteous judgment on persistent unbelief. Grace and judgment both display His freedom.
basic.crossReferences: 1 Kings 19 records Elijah's despair and God's reply about the seven thousand. Isaiah 10 promises that a remnant will return. Romans 9 has already introduced God's electing purpose. Ephesians 2 declares salvation by grace, not works, so no one may boast.
basic.oneStory: Through the whole Old Testament, judgment never wiped out God's people entirely; a remnant always survived by grace. Here Paul shows that same pattern continuing in his own day and in himself. The story of God keeping a people for Himself runs straight on into the church, and it stands on grace from first to last.

context.literary: Chapter 11 opens the final movement of Paul's three-chapter argument about Israel, chapters 9 to 11. Verses 1 to 10 answer the question raised by chapters 9 and 10: given Israel's unbelief, has God rejected His people? The paragraph moves from Paul's own case, to the Elijah precedent, to the principle of grace, to a catena of Scripture describing hardening.
context.historical: Paul writes to a mixed church in Rome where Gentile believers may have begun to look down on unbelieving Israel and to assume God was finished with the Jews. Writing as a Jewish believer himself, Paul insists that God's commitment to His people endures, embodied in the remnant of Jewish Christians of whom he is one.
context.canonical: The remnant is a deep biblical theme, from Noah's family, to the seven thousand under Elijah, to the returning exiles, to the believing Jews of Paul's day. Romans 11 gathers this thread and sets it within the doctrine of election by grace, showing the continuity of God's saving purpose across both Testaments.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To answer decisively that God has not rejected His people, to ground that answer in the biblical pattern of a remnant preserved by grace, to establish that grace and works cannot both be the basis of salvation, and to account for the unbelief of the majority as a divine hardening under judgment.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The passage describes God's dealings with Israel past and present. Its abiding truths are prescriptive: God is faithful to His covenant people, salvation is always by grace and never by works, and hardening is the sobering fruit of persistent unbelief.

debates.question: What does it mean that God foreknew His people in verse 2?

views.label: Foreknowledge as covenant love set beforehand
views.case: The historic and Reformed reading takes foreknew as God's setting of His covenant love upon a people in advance, an electing knowledge, consistent with Romans 8:29; He has not rejected those He chose to love.

views.label: Foreknowledge as advance awareness of faith
views.case: Some read it as God knowing beforehand who would believe; yet in Scripture to know a people carries the sense of covenant relationship, not bare prediction.

debates.bearing: On the historic reading, the security of the remnant rests on God's prior choice, not on foreseen merit, which is why His rejection of them is unthinkable and grace remains grace.

debates.question: Is the hardening of the rest God's act or their own?

views.label: God's righteous judicial hardening
views.case: The text says God gave them a spirit of stupor, presenting the hardening as God's righteous judgment, foregrounded here as the sober biblical teaching that God hands the persistently unbelieving over to their chosen blindness.

views.label: Purely self-inflicted hardening
views.case: Some stress that Israel hardened itself by unbelief; this is true as far as it goes, but it does not do justice to the active language of God giving them a spirit of stupor.

debates.bearing: Held together, human unbelief is real and culpable while God's judicial hardening is just; yet Paul will show this hardening is neither total, for there is a remnant, nor final, for mercy is God's deeper aim.

study.originalLanguages: The paragraph turns on four weighty words. Israel as a whole missed what it sought, but the ekloge, the elect, obtained it, because God's choice, not human effort, is decisive. Those who remain are the leimma, the remnant, kept as surely as Elijah's seven thousand. They are chosen by charis, grace, and Paul presses the logic hard: if by grace, then not by works, for the two cannot mix without grace ceasing to be grace. Meanwhile the rest were subject to poroo, a hardening that dulled eyes and ears, a real judgment yet, as the chapter will reveal, not the last word.

typology.fulfillment: The faithful who never bowed to Baal foreshadow the remnant of grace in every generation, kept by God when the faithful seem to stand alone.
The preserved few of the prophets point forward to the believing remnant of Jewish Christians and, in Christ, to the whole church kept by grace.
Paul, saved by grace, and every believing Jew embody the pattern fulfilled perfectly in Christ, the true Israel in whom the remnant is gathered.

study.christ: Paul stands before us as living proof of grace, an Israelite once zealous against Christ, now His apostle because the risen Lord chose him. The remnant is a remnant in Christ, chosen by the grace that flows from Him. What Israel sought, righteousness and acceptance with God, is found only in Him, obtained by the elect through faith and never by works. Even the hardening points to Christ, for it fell on those who stumbled over Him, the stone of stumbling and rock of offense.
study.god: God is gloriously faithful: He has not rejected the people He foreknew, and His word has not failed. He is sovereign in grace, reserving a remnant for Himself as He did in Elijah's day, so that salvation rests on His free choice. He is also holy and just, and the hardening of the unbelieving majority is His righteous judgment. The passage guards both the freeness of His grace and the seriousness of His justice.

crossReferences.note: Elijah despairs that he alone is left; God reveals He has kept seven thousand who never bowed to Baal.
A remnant of Israel will return and lean on the LORD, the biblical root of the remnant theme.
God's electing purpose stands: not all descended from Israel are Israel, and God's choice, not works, decides.
Salvation is by grace through faith, not by works, so that no one can boast, echoing the logic of verse 6.

study.oneStory: In every age of the Bible, God's judgments never emptied the world of His people; a remnant always came through by grace, from Noah to Elijah's seven thousand to the returning exiles. Paul shows that same faithful pattern alive in his own day and in himself. The long story of God keeping a people for His own name flows on into the church, resting from beginning to end on grace and not on works.

quiz.q: How does Paul answer the question, did God reject His people?

options.text: Certainly not; God has preserved a remnant, and Paul himself is proof.
options.note: Paul denies it emphatically and points to himself and to the remnant of grace.

options.text: Yes, God has finished with Israel and turned only to the Gentiles.
options.note: Paul flatly denies this; God has not rejected the people He foreknew.

options.text: Partly; God rejected the leaders but kept the ordinary people.
options.note: The division Paul draws is between the remnant of grace and the hardened, not leaders and people.

options.text: God has only postponed His rejection to a later day.
options.note: Paul speaks of present preservation, a remnant at the present time, not a delayed rejection.

quiz.q: What does the example of Elijah teach about the remnant?

options.text: Even when the faithful seem alone, God has kept a people for Himself by grace.
options.note: God had reserved seven thousand, just as He now keeps a remnant chosen by grace.

options.text: That faithful people are always a visible majority.
options.note: The remnant is the preserved few, not the impressive majority.

options.text: That God rescues only those who first prove their loyalty.
options.note: God reserved them for Himself; the emphasis is His keeping, not their prior proving.

options.text: That prophets are usually mistaken about their times.
options.note: Elijah misjudged how many were left, but the point is God's faithful preservation, not prophetic error.

quiz.q: Why does Paul say that if it is by grace, then it is no longer by works?

options.text: Because grace and works are opposite grounds; to mix them destroys grace.
options.note: Verse 6 states that if salvation were by works, grace would no longer be grace.

options.text: Because works come later, after grace has begun the process.
options.note: Paul is speaking of the ground of standing before God, which is grace alone, not a sequence.

options.text: Because good works have no place at all in the Christian life.
options.note: Works have their place as fruit; Paul's point is that they are not the basis of election.

options.text: Because grace is simply God rewarding our best efforts.
options.note: That would make grace a wage; Paul insists grace is free and unearned.

quiz.q: What happened to those in Israel who were not part of the remnant?

options.text: They were hardened, given eyes that could not see and ears that could not hear.
options.note: Verses 7 to 10 describe the hardening of the rest under God's righteous judgment.

options.text: They were immediately and finally destroyed.
options.note: Paul speaks of hardening, and later shows it is neither total nor final.

options.text: They were left completely neutral, neither helped nor hindered.
options.note: Scripture describes an active hardening, a spirit of stupor, not neutrality.

options.text: They were saved anyway apart from faith.
options.note: The hardened did not obtain what Israel sought; only the elect did.

quiz.q: On what basis is the remnant chosen?

options.text: By grace, God's free and unmerited favor, not by their works.
options.note: Paul says the remnant is chosen by grace, and grace excludes works.

options.text: By their superior obedience to the law.
options.note: If it were by works it would no longer be grace; obedience is not the basis.

options.text: By their descent from Abraham alone.
options.note: Physical descent is not enough; the remnant is chosen by grace, as Romans 9 has shown.

options.text: By a mixture of grace and personal merit.
options.note: Paul rules out any mixture; if it is by grace it is no longer by works.

questions: Paul points to himself as proof that God has not given up on His people. Where do you need to remember that God is faithful even when the situation looks hopeless?
Elijah thought he was the only faithful one left, but God had kept seven thousand. How does it encourage you to know that God is always preserving His people, even when you cannot see them?
The remnant is chosen by grace, not works. How would resting in God's free choice, rather than your own performance, change the way you relate to Him?
Verse 6 says grace and works cannot both be the ground of salvation. What are you tempted to add to grace as if it were not enough?
The hardening of the rest is a sobering judgment on unbelief. How does this move you to pray for those who cannot yet see, and to guard your own heart from becoming dull to God's word?

translationNotes.note: Whom He foreknew renders a verb that in Scripture means more than advance awareness; it carries the sense of God setting His covenant love on a people beforehand, an electing knowledge like that of Romans 8:29.

Some later manuscripts (BYZ and TR) add a second clause, But if it is by works, then it is no longer grace; otherwise work is no longer work. The BSB follows the earlier text; the sense is unchanged, that grace and works are mutually exclusive as grounds of salvation.
The quotation blends Deuteronomy 29:4 and Isaiah 29:10. A spirit of stupor renders a word for numbness or deep sleep; God gives the persistently unbelieving over to spiritual insensibility.

Footnote markers (noteId) were skipped and line breaks rendered as spaces; a space was preserved where a footnote marker fell between two sentences (verse 11 context). No em or en dashes occur in this passage. Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon URL verified this session.
